text: FLADUNGEN, a small town of Bavaria, in the circle of Lower Franconia, on a river of the same name, 40 m. N of Wurzburg. Pop. 710.
FLAGG, a township in the p. of Bakewell, in Derby, 51 m. W of Bakewell. Pop. in 1831, 232; in 1841, 249.
FLAGNAC, a small town of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, 6 m. N by E of Albin. Pop. 1,938.
FLAMANVILLE, a seaport of France, in the dep. of La Manche, 14 m. SW of Cherburg. Pop. 1,204.

FLAMBOROUGH, a parish and village in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. ENE of Bridlington. The v. is situated in the centre of a promontory on the North sea. It was anciently a place of some consequence, but has dwindled into little more than a fishing-vil- FLA lage. Area of p. 2,980 acres. Pop. in 1831, 975; in 1841, 1,191.

FLAMBOROUGH-HEAD, a magnificent range of limestone rocks, extending along the Yorkshire coast for several miles, and in some places rising perpendicularly to the height of 150 yds. During the summer season, these cliffs are the resort of immense numbers of birds, chiefly sea-fowl, which breed in the high and inaccessible crevices of the rocks. For many years the want of a lighthouse here was severely felt by mariners and merchants. From June 1770 to the end of 1806, when the present light was first exhibited, not fewer than 174 ships were wrecked or lost on F.-head and its environs. The site of the lighthouse is on the cliff nearly 1½ m. E of the town, about 400 yds. within the extreme point of the promontory, close to the landing on the S side of Silex bay, and at an elevation of 250 ft., in N lat. 54° 7', W long. 0° 6'. The light is revolving; and in order to distinguish it from the revolving lights of Tinmouth and Cromar, showing a face every minute, this light was made to exhibit a face every two minutes, one of them coloured red. The interval of time between them is 2 min. and 4 min. alternately, sufficiently distinguishing this light from any other on the British coasts. From this light those at Cromer, Spurn-point, and the Dudgeon, bear by the compass S 101, E dist. 293 leagues, S by W 11 leagues, and about S 13° E 21½ respectively, the variation being 25° W of the true meridian.

FLAMBOROUGH (East and West), two townships in Upper Canada; the former in the Gore district, to the N of Burlington bay, the latter adjacent to it on the SW, in Wentworth co. Pop. of East F. in 1841, 1,341; of West F. 2,428. In the latter township is the v. of F., 7 m. from Hamilton. Pop.
FLAMENGRIE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of La Chapelle. Pop. 1,744.
FLAMSTEAD, a parish of Hertfordshire, 3 m. NNW of Redbourne. Area 5,660 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,392; in 1841, 1,492.

FLANDERS, a village of Southampton township, Suffolk co., in the state of New York, U. S., 229 m. SE of Albany, on the S side of Little Peconic bay, consisting in 1840 of about 15 houses.—Also a village of Roxburgh township, Morris co., in the state of New Jersey, 55 m. NE of Trenton, at the E foot of Schooley's mountain, consisting of about 30 dwellings.
FLANDERS, formerly a province and district of France, of which Lille was capital, now comprised in the dep. of the Nord.

FLANDERS (East), a province of Belgium, chiefly composed of the E part of the ancient county of Flanders; bounded on the N by Holland, from which it is separated by an irregular frontier-line; on the E by the provs. of Antwerp and Brabant; on the S by the prov. of Hainault; and on the W by W. Flanders. Its surface, which lies between the parallels of 50° 42′ and 51° 22′ N, is a plain, interrupted by some small hills declining towards the W and N. Its average length from N to S is about 40 m.; its breadth 32 m. The soil is fertile, and well-cultivated; even the hills have a good soil and excellent pastures. The total amount of productive land is estimated at 268,600 hect. The prov. of East F. is remarkably favoured by nature with regard to water-carriage. It is traversed by four principal rivers, the Scheldt, the Dendre, the Darme, and the Lys. There are also numerous smaller streams, which are used more or less for the purposes of transit; but the great glory of the prov. is in its canals, -stupendous monuments of the munificent enterprise of the earlier Flemings, which still facilitate the operations of a trade once the largest in the world. Every traveller knows the canal from Bruges to Ghent; besides this, there is another of these great artificial arteries connecting Ghent with the Neuzen canal; and there is also the Moerwart canal. The pop. of the prov. in 1815 was 521,466; in 1840, 641,127; in 1848 it was 781,143, of whom 202,700 inhabited the principal towns, 13,204 were of French or Walloon descent, and 779,463 of Flemish or Dutch. The agriculture of this prov. is admirable, and the richest harvests in the kingdom are raised here, though the soil is chiefly of a sandy nature. It would, however, "be a mistake to suppose that what is generally known as Flemish agriculture prevails exclusively throughout the two provs. of East and West F. It is only in particular districts, such as the Pays-de-Waes, the environs of Ghent and Bruges, and some other large towns, that you find the extraordinary minuteness of cultivation, under the personal superintendence of the cultivator, which has procured for Flemish agriculture its distinctive character. In the N parts of the two provs. are situated 'the Polders,' and in the neighbourhood of Dixmude there are extensive pasturages for the rearing of cattle. There are also, in different parts of the two provs., many thousand hectares of waste land, and a considerable quantity of peat and bog land." [Morning Chronicle.] The surface of East F., containing 299,789 hectares, or 799,473 acres, was divided, in 1830, into 802,263 allotments, belonging to 137,791 proprietors. The average size of properties was therefore 5 and 11-13th acres; that of the allotments was under 1 acre each. In 1850 the total number of proprietors in the prov. was 111,479; of whom there were 9,903 with an annual revenue from land not exceeding 10 francs; 19,311 with a revenue of 10 to 25 f.; 20,834 from 25 to 50 f.; 20,723 from 50 to 100 f.; 11,498 from 200 to 400 f.; 9,487 from 400 to 600 f.; 2,351 from 600 to 800 f.; and 1,351 from 600 to 1,000 f. One proprietor had above 100,000 f. of yearly revenue from land; 3 from 70,000 to 100,000 f.; and 10 from 50,000 to 70,000 f. The inhabitants are Roman Catholics, and speak Flemish. French is generally spoken in the towns, and is likewise understood in a great part of the country. spread over the country; for although, in Ghent itself, the modern manufacturing system has found dustrial force is exceptional. Almost every town or v. in this prov. has its little hive of manufacturing hand-labour; while, in every peasant's cottage, the loom, the spinning-wheel, or the lace-board, divides pation of the country-people after the field-work was done. There were in 1842, in East F., 31,697 looms employed in weaving flax, 6,124 for cotton, and 639 for mixed stuffs. Audenarde and St. Nicholas are the districts in which flax is chiefly cultivated; the latter alone contains 3,800 bonniers sown with it, which yield about 2,000,000 lbs. a-year, onethird of it being exported to England and elsewhere. It is in the cottages of the small proprietors, owning from 3 to 5 bonniers, that the machines are mostly in motion, because they employ the evenings in a kind of work which it is often not worth while for farmers to hire people to do. Of late years, however, the Belgian spinning and weaving mills have hopelessly ruined the small hand spinners and weavers; while Flanders too, like Ireland, suffers from an excess of pauper occupancy. The farms are generally from 2 to 20 acres in extent, and handlabour prevails. The cultivation of flax is carried to a high degree of perfection in this prov., espe- -cially in the Pays-de-Waes, between Antwerp and Ghent. This prov. is divided into 3 judicial arrondissements: viz. Ghent, Audenarde, and Termonde; and 6 administrative arrond., viz. Alost, Audenarde, Eccloo, Ghent, Termonde, and Saint-Nicholas. It is subdivided into 33 cantons, 282 rural communes, and 11 towns. Besides its capital city, Ghent, which ranks next to Brussels itself in importance, there are other considerable towns in East F., such as Alost, Deynze, Eecloo, with woollen and cotton manufactories and distilleries; Lokeren, where also are manufactories of cotton, linen, lace, and hats, and breweries, tanneries, dye-houses, and bleaching-grounds; Saint- Nicholas, with its large flax-market; and Audenarde, a great place for linen manufacture, and for other industrial operations.

FLANDERS (West), a prov. of Belgium; bounded on the NW and N by the North sea; on the E by E. Flanders and Hainault; on the S and SW by France. The surface of this prov. is level; but some rising downs occur along the coast. In the interior, the soil is heavy and fertile; in other places it is sandy, and exhibits numerous heaths and marshes, which have been made fit for cultivation, however, by the aid of canals. The principal rivers are the Scheldt, the Lys, and the Yser. The principal canals are those from Ghent to Bruges, from Bruges to Ostend, and from Ostend to Nieuport. The climate is changeable, and occasions, particularly in the N and W districts, frequent agues. The country is cultivated like a garden; and the great variety of the productions of the soil breaks that uniformity which extensive corn-fields generally exhibit. The land between Bruges and Ghent resembles the market-gardens between Hammersmith and Brentford, in the neighbourhood of London: for 40 m. you pass through a succession of rich garden-land, interspersed with corn, rye, and flax-fields. The following table, drawn up in 1834, shows the nature and distribution of the soil at that period: Hectares. Acres. 1. Arable land: The above table shows that there is a certain quantity of land not bearing any produce, but much labour is daily bestowed on the cultivation of this land. The unproductive land may be divided into two classes,—the heaths and the lands left by the sea, which latter, when drained, are called polders. The heaths might all be made productive; and many proprietors have already turned their attention to the cultivation of them. The polders can only be cultivated by large capitalists, who can afford to lose the interest of their outlay for several years. The returns are finally very considerable. This sort of speculation is frequently tried in the Fs.—The soil here is a rich, black, vegetable mould over a light sandy subsoil, so light and friable in texture that it is turned by the spade, without using the foot. One horse is sufficient for the plough, and for the harrow, which has coarse wooden teeth, and is generally driven by a boy or a man. The country is divided into small farms of from 2 to 100 acres, usually the property of the occupant. The larger farms are chiefly situated in the N of the prov.; there are some containing as much as 300 measures, or 325 acres, but the polders form a large proportion of them. The average extent of farms is from 50 to 70 measures, or 54 to 76 acres. There are some also varying from 20 to 25 measures, or from 211 to 27 acres. It is frequently the case that the whole farm does not belong to the same proprietor. The tenant, in order to enlarge his farm, rents from 5 to 12 measures off small proprietors, which are called loopende landen, or 'running lands.' As there is every variety of soil in the prov., from the best to the worst in all Europe, an average rate of the rent will hardly give any idea of the respective value of the land. The highest price of grass meadows, i. e. those used for grazing, varies from 100 to 125 francs per hectare, or from £1 12s. to £2 per acre. The rent for arable land varies from 15 f. to 56 f. per hectare, or from 5s. to 19s. per acre, on account of the great difference in the quality. The average rate of rent is 25 f. per hectare, or 8s. per acre. Wheat produces from 16 to 18 double hectolitres per hectare, or from 34 to 38 bushels per acre; colza (Brassica arvensis), or rapeseed, 14 double hectolitres per hectare, or 30 bushels per acre; rye, the same; barley, 24 double hectolitres per hectare, or 50 bushels per acre; oats, 16 double hectolitres per hectare, or 34 bushels per acre; potatoes, 200 sacks of 100 kilogrammes each per hectare, or 157 cwt. per acre; flax, which is only produced on very good soil, returns a value of from 600 f. to 700 f. per hectare, or £9 12s. to £11 4s. per acre. [Consular returns in 1834.] West F. contained in 1842, in a superficies of 799,422 acres, no less than 656,604 registered properties, which consequently average but 14 acre each; but the number of proprietors is 61,355. Of these 3,917 have an yearly revenue of 10 francs and under from land; 10,324 of from 25 to 50 f.; 10,987 of from 50 to 100 f.; 9,324 of from 100 to 200 f.; 7,668 of from 200 to 400 f.; 3,380 of from 400 to 600 f.; 2 persons from 70,000 to 100,000 f.; 11 from 25,000 to 50,000 f.; and 22 from 20,000 to 25,000 f. In the neighbourhood of the coast, towns. farms and possessions are larger than in the interior. The commune of Furnes counts 24 acres to an inhabitant; the commune of Ostend has one inhabitant on 13 acre. In the commune of Bruges there is one inhabitant to 1½ acre, and in that of Courtray one to 7-10ths of an acre. "By so dense a pop. every check that the other branches of industry experience naturally throws the inhabitants upon the land; and if the landlord's interest really lay in pasture. having the land thus morselled out, matters must stand better with the Belgian landlords than with any others." The cottage of the agricultural labourer in this prov. is generally built of brick, and consists of two or three rooms: it is frequently his nosand. own, as well as the small garden in which it stands. The diet of the peasantry is chiefly rye-bread, an abundant supply of vegetables, soup, a little meat, eggs, cheese, coffee, and beer. Wages average 15d. per day in summer, and 10d. in winter. The burdens on the land are light; but the taxation in towns, including general and municipal duties, is heavy. "From time immemorial it has been the practice of the Flemish peasant to join some small manufacturing industry to his small agricultural occupation. The wife and the daughter spin; the husband and the sons weave. All take their turn, from time to time, in the labour of the little farm; the hours that cannot be employed in the field or the garden being devoted to the spinning-wheel and the loom. This may be regarded as the normal condi- FLA tion of the Belgian peasant cultivator in the two provs. of East and West F.; and, notwithstanding the decline of the linen manufacture, which once formed the glory and the wealth of these fertile provinces, and which contributed, with their agriculture, to make them the richest districts in Europe, the inhabitants still cling to the ancient usage with that tenacity which forms the chief feature of the Flemish character. In the present day, although the agriculture of these provs. still holds its place, and yields in proportion more largely than the rest of the kingdom, their manufacturing industry has fallen off." [Morning Chronicle.] The linen produced in the environs of Courtrai is known over all Europe. Table-linen, lace, thread, cotton, and woollen-cloth, camlets, and serges, are also made to a great extent; and the commerce is very animated. As a means of alleviating the suffering caused by the decline of the linen manufacture, government has established in the rural communes ateliers where female children are taught to make lace. The pop. in 1815 was 521,464; in 1840, 641,127; in 1848, 626,847, of whom 182,764 inhabited the principal towns; 34,380 were of French or Walloon descent, and 607,413 of Dutch or Flemish. The inhabitants are zealous Roman Catholics; but an honest, frank, and industrious race. They have a turn for painting and music. French is almost generally spoken. The number of primary schools in 1839 was 608 with 59,687 pupils. The prov. is divided into 4 judicial arrondissements, viz., Bruges, Courtrai, Ypres, and Furnes; and 8 administrative arrondissements, viz., Bruges, Ostend, Dixmude, Furnes, Ypres, Courtrai, Thielt, and Roulers. These were subdivided into 32 cant. and 233 com. The chief town is Bruges. Ostend is 16 m. W of Bruges, and 22 m. NE of Dunkirk. Nieuport and Ypres are fortified towns. Courtrai, on the Lys, is a place of animated industry and trade. The linen, which takes its name from this town, is celebrated for its fineness, and is fabricated in the surrounding country, whence about 30,000 pieces are annually brought to market. Furnes is a large mart for agricultural produce. Roulers, Thielt, Thorout, and Dixmude, are all active manufacturing
FLANDRE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, cant. of Naast. Pop. 209.
FLANIZ, a river of Austria, in the prov. of Lower Austria, circle of the Wiener-Walde, which falls into the Danube to the E of Mautern.
FLANNAN ISLES, a group of uninhabited islands in the group of the Hebrides, 15 m. NW of Gallan-head, in Lewis. They afford excellent sheep
FLANSHAM, a hamlet in the p. of Felpham, co. of Sussex. Pop. 76.
FLAS, or Flasjon, a lake of Sweden, in the laen of Ostersund, near the confines of the laen of Her-
FLASBY WITH WINTERBURN, a township in the p. of Gargrave, W. R. of Yorkshire, 41 m. NNW of Skipton. Area 2,940 acres. Pop. 140.
FLASH, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, ligue des Dix Droitures, on the Rhine, to the NNW of Mayenfield. Pop. 495.
FLASHBROOK, a township in the p. of Adbaston, Staffordshire, 31 m. N of Newport. Pop. 109.
FLASSANS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Var, cant. of Besse. Pop. 1,254. FLAT - BOW, KOOTANIE, OF M'GILLIVRAY

RIVER, a river which takes its rise in the Rocky mountains, British North America, to the NE of the source of the Columbia river; runs SSE into Oregon; passes Kootanie fort; thence bends WNW, re-enters the British territory, and expands into a considerable lake of the same name. On issuing thence it runs about 60 m. to the Columbia river, which it joins at the S side of Arrow lake. A portage of 21 m. connects this river with the source of the Columbia.
FLAT-CREEK, a township of Taney co., in the state of Missouri, U. S. Pop. in 1840, 181.—Also a township of Pettis co., in the same state. Pop. 621.
FLAT-HOLM, an island belonging to the p. and 8 m. WNW of Uphill, Somerset. It is about 11 m. in circumference. On its highest point is a lighthouse with a revolving light.

FLAT-ISLAND, an island of the Mergui archipelago, near the W coast of the isthmus of Krah, in N lat. 10° 27'. It is about 1 m. in diam., and produces fruit, yams, plantains, and forest trees of large dimensions.—Also an island in the bay of Bengal, near the coast of Aracan, and SE of the island of Cheduba, in N lat. 18° 37'.
FLAT-ISLAND, or MOTIN, an island of New Zealand, near the NE coast of New Ulster, in Lowland bay, in S lat. 37° 45′, and E long. 176° 25′.

FLAT ISLANDS, two small islands, 81 m. from the W coast of Sumatra, and to the SE of Hog island, in N lat. 2° 20', and E long. 96° 5'. They are well-wooded, and produce considerable quantities of cocoa-nut oil, which, with edible birds'-nests, form their chief article of export. Buffaloes and pigs abound in the woods.

FLAT-POINT, a headland of Sumatra, at the SW extremity of the island, and at the entrance of the strait of Sunda, in S lat. 6°, and E long. 104° 20'. —Also a promontory on the S coast of Borneo, in S lat. 3° 30', and E long. 112° 10'.
FLAT-ROCK, a township of Henry co., in the state of Ohio, U. S. Pop. in 1840, 475.—Also a village of Shelby co., in the state of Indiana, 61. m. SE of Indianapolis, on a creek of the same name.

FLATBUSH, a township of King's co., in the state of New York, U. S., 151 m. S of Albany, and 5 m. SE of York. It is generally level, and possesses a fine light loamy soil. Pop. in 1840, 2,099. It is noted for the battle of Flatbush or of Long island, fought in 1776 near its NW boundary. The village is pleasantly situated on a plain 4 m. SE of Brooklyn. Pop. 400.
FLATHEAD-HOUSE, an establishment in Oregon, on the r. bank of Flathead or Clark's river, 150 m. E of the confluence of that river with the Columbia.
FLATHEAD RIVER. See CLARK'S RIVER.
FLATHEAD, or Selish (Lake), a small sheet of water in Oregon, which discharges itself into the river of the same name, 30 m. W of Flathead-House.
FLATHEADS, a tribe of North American Indians, who inhabit the banks of the river to which they give their name, in Oregon, between 45° and 49° N lat,, and 111° and 116° W long.
FLATLANDS, a township of King's co., in the state of New York, 8 m. SE of Brooklyn. Its surface is level, and its soil sandy loam. Pop. in 1840, inclusive of Barren island, 810. Pop. of village, 100.

FLATOW, or Czlorowo, a circle and town of Prussia, in the prov. of Prussia, regency and 89 m. WSW of Marienwerder, between 3 lakes. Pop. 2,320. It has a Catholic and a Lutheran church, and a synagogue, and possesses manufactories of lace and cloth. Its trade consists chiefly in corn. The circle of F. contains 26,777 inhabitants.

FLATTERY (CAPE), a headland of North East Australia, on the NE coast, in S lat. 14° 55', and E long. 145° 15'.—Also a headland of Oregon, at the entrance of the strait of Juan-de-Fuca, in N lat. 48° 40'.
FLAUNDEN, a chapelry in the p. of Hemel- Hempstead, Hertfordshire, 51/4 m. WSW of King's Langby. Area 970 acres. Pop. 295. FLE
FLAUNE, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, cant. of Bodignee, 6 m. NE of Huy, on the l. bank of the Meuse. Pop. 126. It has extensive alum refineries.
FLAVEL-FLYFORD, or FLYFORD-FLAVEL, a parish in Worcestershire, 8 m. E of Worcester. Area 730 acres. Pop. 156.

FLAVIGNAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lot, cant. of Castelnau-de-Montratier. Pop. 1,159.—Also a commune in the dep. of Haut-Vienne, cant. of Chalus, 18 m. NNW of Saint-Yrieix. Pop. 1,528.

FLAVIGNY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Meurthe, cant. of Saint-Nicolas-du-Port. Pop, 1,216.—Also a canton, commune, and town, in the dep. of the Côte-d'Or, arrond. of Semur. The cant. comprises 23 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,759; in 1841, 12,079. The town is situated at the foot of a mountain, on the r. bank of the Ozerain, 10 m. E of Semur. It has a considerable trade in corn, wool, and anise. Fairs are held five times a-year. Sheep of the merino breed are extensively reared in the locality.
FLAVION, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, arrond. and 18 m. NE of Philippeville. Pop. 624. It has considerable iron-mines.
FLAVY-LE-MARTEL, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Aisne, cant. of Saint-Simon, 12 m. SSW of Saint-Quentin. Pop. 2,366.
FLAWBOROUGH, a chapelry in the p. of Staunton, Nottinghamshire, 7 m. SSW of Newark. Area 920 acres. Pop. 79.

FLAWEIL, or FLAWYL, a parish and village of Switzerland, cap. of the bail. of New-Toggenburg, in the cant. and 11 m. W of Saint Gullen, on the road to Zurich. Pop. 1,000. It is well-built, and has considerable manufactories of muslin and calico. In the vicinity is a Cistercian convent.
FLAWINNE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov., cant., and 12 m. W of Namur, on the l. bank of the Sambre. Pop. 1,656. It has manufactories of tobacco, and considerable coal and iron mines.
FLAWITH, a township in the p. of Alne, N. R. or Yorkshire, 43 m. SW of Easingwold, and near the Great North of England railway. Area 680 acres. Pop. 90.
FLAXWEILER, a village of Belgium, in the prov. and 11 m. ENE of Luxemburg. Pop. 1,238.
FLAX-BOURTON, or BOURTON, a parish in Somerset, 55 m. WSW of Bristol, intersected by the Bristol and Exeter railway. Area 540 acres. Pop. 232.
FLAXBY, a township in the p. of Goldsborough, W. R. of Yorkshire, 3\frac{1}{2} m. ENE of Knaresborough. Area 580 acres. Pop. 102.
FLAXLEY, a parish of Gloucestershire, 2<sup>1</sup>/<sub>4</sub> m. N of Newnham. Area 1,980 acres. Pop. 229.
FLAXPOOL, a hamlet in the p. of Crowcombe, Somerset. Pop. 52.
FLAXTON-ON-THE-MOOR, a township, partly in the p. of Bossall, and partly in that of Foston, N. R. of Yorkshire, 9 m. NNE of York. Area 1,460 acres. Pop. 412.
FLAYAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Creuse, cant. of Crocq, 18 m. SE of Aubusson. Pop. 1,010.
FLAYOSC, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Var, cant. of Draguignan. Pop. in 1841, 2,660.
FLECHAS POINT, a headland of the island of Mindanao, Asiatic archipelago, on the SW coast, on the W side of Illana bay, in N lat. 7° 15', E long. 123° 5'.

FLECHE (LA), an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe. -The arrond. comprises an area of 160,199 hectares, and contains 7 cant., viz.: Brulow, La Fleche, Le Lude, Malicorne, Mayet, Pont-Valin, and Sablé. Pop. in 1831, 96,320; in 1846, 101,926.—The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 18,394; in 1841, 19,437.—The town is situated in a fine valley, on the r. bank of the Loir, 27 m. SW of Le Mans. Pop. in 1789, 3,848; in 1821, 5,387; in 1831, 6,421; in 1841, 7,009. It is well-built, and is adorned with several fine promenades. Its principal buildings are the theatre and the military school. The latter is a very handsome edifice, with an elegant church, a library, a gallery of paintings, and is surrounded by an extensive park, gardens, and other pleasure-grounds. It is supplied with water by an aqueduct, 3 m. in extent. This structure was originally a castle, built by Henry IV., and subsequently a Jesuit college. F. has manufactories of glue, and several wax-works and tanneries; but, notwithstanding the advantages of its situation, it possesses but little trade, fowls, dried fruit, and grain, being its chief articles of export. Fairs are held monthly.—F. is noted as the birth-place of Des Cartes, of the abbot Picard, and of Louis Sauveur. Previous to the 10th cent. this town bore the name of Fissa. It received its present appellation from a lofty spire erected by Count Helie on the church of St. Thomas. It has been the seat of several civil wars.
FLECHERE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, cant. of Gony-lez-Pieton. Pop. 509.

FLECKEROE, or FLEKKERÖE, an island of Norway, near the S coast, in the stift and 4 m. S of stone. Christiansund, in N lat. 58° 10'. The narrow passage by which it is separated from the continent forms an excellent port.
FLECKNEY, a parish of Leicestershire, 7 m. NW of Market-Harborough, intersected by the Union canal. Area 1,630 acres. Pop. 473.
FLEDBOROUGH, a parish of Nottinghamshire, 54 m. ENE of Tuxford, on the N bank of the Trent. Area 1,300 acres. Pop. 112.
FLEDERBORN, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Pomerania, circle of Koslin, SE of Neu-Stettin. Pop. 250.
FLE'E-SAINTE-CECILE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe, cant. of Chateau-du-Loir. Pop. 1,158.

FLEET, a parish in Dorset, 4 m. WNW of Weymouth. Area 1,400 acres. Pop. 140.—Also a p. of Lincolnshire, 2 m. ESE of Holbeach. Area 6,710 acres. Pop. 960.—Also a small river in the stewartry of Kirkeudbrightshire, which issues from a lake of the same name, and flows SSE into Wigtonbay, expanding into a considerable estuary.—Also a small river in Nottinghamshire, an affluent of the Trent.—Also an arm of the sea on the SE coast of Sutherlandshire, across which the high road is carried over the mouth of Strathfleet, upon an embankment 995 yds. in length.
FLEETHAM, a township in the p. of Bambrough, Northumberland, 6 m. SSE of Belford. Pop. 52.

FLEETWOOD-ON-WYRE, a maritime town and watering-place in the p. of Poulton-le-Fyle, Laneashire, on the Wyre, at its entrance into Morecombe bay, in N lat. 53° 55½, W long. 3° 1', and 18 m. NW of Preston, with which it is connected by the Preston and Wyre railway. Steamers now ply between this port and the Isle of Man, Whitehaven, Dublin, Belfast, Ardrossan, Ulverston, Dundalk, and Newry. Its fine harbour and extensive dock, its easy and safe approach to the sea, the lowness of its portdues and dock-charges, and its connection by railway with the great manufacturing districts, cannot, it is thought, fail ultimately to attract a large proportion of the foreign trade to this port. There is a customs' establishment here, with bonded ware- III. enitaresessessessessessessessessesses FLE houses for all goods except E. India goods and tobacco. The town now contains a handsome church, a large hotel, and a market. At the entrance seaward a screw-pile lighthouse has been reared, besides two shore lighthouses. The landing-wharf presents a frontage of 600 ft., with a depth of about 300, and a line of rails to the main railway. The pop. in 1841 was 2,833.

FLEHINGEN, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Middle Rhine, bail, and 6 m. NE of Bretten, on the Krainbach, at an alt. of 360 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,138, of whom 523 are Catholics, and 137 Jews,

FLEIMSER-THAL, a valley of Tyrol, in the circle and NE of Trent. It is about 33 m. in length, and is traversed in its entire extent by the Avisio, which falls into the Adige 31 m. W of Lavis. It contains about 11,180 inhabitants.
FLEIX (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Dordogne, cant. of La Force, 13 m. W of Bergerac. Pop. 600.

FLEKKEFIORD, a maritime town of Norway, in the stift and 54 m. W of Christiansund, bail. and 37 m. WNW of Mandal, on the Lal-fiord, an arm of the German sea. Pop. 873. It has a church and a custom-house. The construction of timber houses, and the adjacent herring-fisheries, form the branches of local industry.
FLEMALLE (GRAND), a commune of Belgium, in the prov. and 6 m. SW of Liege, on the Meuse. Pop. 1,256. It has quarries of paving and grinding-
FLEMHUDE, a village of Holstein, 6 m. W of Kiel, on the r. bank of the embouchure of the Eyder in the Holstein or Kieler canal.

FLEMING, a county of the state of Kentucky, U. S., comprising an area of 570 sq. m., bordered on the SW by Licking river. It is in some parts mountainous, but in its greater extent possesses a fertile soil, with a substratum of limestone. Pop. in 1840, 13,268. Its cap. is Flemingsburg.—Also a township of Cayuga co., in the state of New York, 4 m. S of Auburn, and 128 m. W of Albany. It has an undulating surface, drained by Crane brook and several small streams, which flow into Owasco lake on its E border. The soil consists of fertile clay loam, and is well-cultivated. Pop. 1,317. The v. contains 150 inhabitants.
FLEMING (CAPE), the NE point of Melville island, on the N coast of Australia.
FLEMINGSBURG, a village of Fleming co., in the state of Kentucky, U. S., 95 m. NE of Frankfort, on Stockton run, 1 m. from its junction with Fleming creek. Pop. in 1840, 591.
FLEMINGSTON, a parish of Glamorganshire, 31 m. SSE of Cowbridge, and 2½ m. N of the Bristol channel. Pop. 74.
FLEMINGTON, a village of Raritan township, Hunterdon co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., 25 m. NW of Trenton, in a valley of considerable fertility. Pop. in 1840, 350.
FLEMPTON, a parish of Suffolk, 5 m, NNW of Bury-St.-Edmunds. Area 720 acres. Pop. in 1801, 99; in 1841, 210.
FLEMUNDA, a town of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Upper Wallachia, on an affluent of the Aluta, about 5 m. from the l. bank of that river, and 40 m. N of Slatina. It has a Greek convent.
FLENSBERG, a parish of Norway, in the dio. of Aggerhuus and bail. of Buskerud, on the l. bank of the Louven, 50 m. W of Christiana. Pop. 2,694.

FLENSBORG, or Flensburg, a maritime town of Denmark, in the duchy and 20 m. N of Sleswig, at the SW extremity of the Flensburg-fiord. Pop. in 1847, inclusive of that of the suburbs, 16,500. It is enclosed by walls and ditches, both much dilapidated, and has 8 gates. It is substantially built, and | well-paved, and is considered one of the finest and best-kept towns in Denmark. Its principal buildings are the churches-of which there are 4-the town-house, custom-house, bank, and theatre. There are also an hospital, an orphan's asylum, and several other charitable institutions; a house-of-correction, a classical academy, a school of navigation, a public library, and a printing-office. The chief articles of local manufacture are sail-cloth and cordage, fine linen, woollen fabrics, paper, tobacco, chicory, Prussian blue, soap, and starch. The town likewise contains extensive sugar-refineries, distilleries of brandy, breweries, and vinegar-manufactories, and several tanneries and dye-works. The trade, which is considerable, consists chiefly in corn, leather, tallow, brandy, and other articles of local manufacture. The environs are fertile. Fairs are held three times a-year. The port which, next to Copenhagen, is the most important in the Danish dominions, was entered by 705 vessels in 1849, of which 48 were English; but by 1,731, of which 88 were English, in 1847; the falling-off being attributable to the war in the duchy. The imports into F. during 1849 consisted chiefly of timber, coals, and other unmanufactured productions; but even these suffered a material decline as compared with former years, owing principally to the inland manufacturers not experiencing any demand for their products on Danish account. The business in colonial produce-of which formerly large quantities were sent to Denmark—was, during 1848 and 1849, almost exclusively restricted to the wants of the duchy, and consequently there was a considerable falling-off in the transatlantic navigation of native vessels, and in the direct imports of colonial produce. In 1847, there were imported into F. from Denmark upwards of 30,000 tierces of seed, for the use of the numerous oil-mills, whilst about 1,500,000 lbs. of oil - the produce of 32,000 tierces of linseed - and 500,000 lbs. of soap, were exported to Denmark. The exports from the duchy of Sleswig to the kingdom of Denmark alone amounted in 1846 to 6,000,000 marks current; and the trade with the Danish colonies was to the extent of 2,000,000 marks more; making a total trade of 8,000,000 marks current in value. To this must be added the imports from Denmark of wool, butter, hides, skins, &c., which of themselves constituted a very profitable business. There were imported by sea into F. during 1849, 76,846 casks of grain and linseed, against 167,481 casks in 1847.
FLERON, a canton, commune, and town of Belgium, in the prov. and 6 m. E of Liege. Pop. 1,180. It has manufactories of cloth and of nails.

FLERS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Orne, arrond. of Domfront.—The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. in 1831, 16,813; in 1841, 20,292.—The town is 12 m. N of Domfront, on the road from Rennes to Caen. Pop. in 1831, 4,386; in 1841, 6,113. It has extensive manufactories of linen, ticking, and varieties of cotton fabrics, and possesses 14 annual fairs.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Launoy, 3 m. E of Lille, Pop. 1,658. It has extensive dye-works and brick-kilns.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Somme, cant. of Ailly-sur-Noye. Pop. 536. FLESH or VLESCH BAY, an indentation of the S coast of South Africa, in George district, in S lat. 34° 15′, and E long. 21° 50′.
FLESK, a river of co. Kerry, which rises 31 m. SW of the co. Cork Mill-street, and, after a course of nearly 20 m., falls into the lake of Killarney 11 m. S of the town of that name.
FLESK (Brown), a rivulet of co. Kerry, which falls into the Main 51 m. above Castlemain. FLE
FLESSELLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Somme, cant. of Villiers-Bocage, 9 m. N of Amiens. Pop. in 1841, 1,693.
FLESSINGUE. See VLISSINGEN.

FLETCHER, a township of Franklin co., in the state of Vermont, U. S., 52 m. NNW of Montpelier, watered by Lamoille and Fairfield's rivers, by the head branch of Black creek, and by Stone's brook. Its surface is irregular, but its soil generally good. Pop. in 1840, 1,014.—Also a village of Brown township, Miami co., in the state of Ohio, 66 m. NW of Columbus. Pop. 150.
FLETCHING, a parish in Sussex, 31 m. NW of Uckfield, and E of the Ouse. Area 5,830 acres. Pop. 1,914.
FLETRE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Bailleul. Pop. 1,184.
FLETTON, a parish of Huntingdonshire, 1 m. SSE of Peterborough. Area 780 acres. Pop. 256.

FLEURANCE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Gers, arrond. of Lectoure. The cant. comprises 19 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,218; in 1841, 12,868.—The town is 7 m. S of Lectoure, on the l. bank of the Gers. Pop. in 1841, 3,409. It has a china manufactory and several tanneries, and possesses an active trade in grain and in feathers. Fairs are held 8 times a-year.
FLEURBAIX, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Laventie. Pop. 3,041.
FLEURIE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhone, cant. of Beaujeu. Pop. 1,874.

FLEURIER, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 18 m. WSW of Neufchatel, in the Val de Travers. Pop. 850. It is well-built, and has extensive manufactories of lace, clocks, and ironmongery. In the vicinity is the Grotte-aux-Fees, one of the finest grottoes in Switzerland.
FLEURIEU BAY. See OYSTER BAY.
FLEURIEU ISLAND. See BARREN ISLAND, Bass strait.
FLEURIGNAT, a village of France, in the dep. of the Charente, cant. of Montambœuf, 23 m. SW of Confolens. Pop. 144. In its vicinity is an iron-mine.
FLEURIGNE', a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. and 4 m. ESE of Fougères. Pop. 1,059. It has some paper-mills.
FLEURINES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Oise, cant. of Sergines, 4 m. N of Senlis. Pop. 563. It has brick and tile kilns.

FLEURUS, or FLEURU, a commune and town of Belgium, in the dep. of Hainault, arrond. and 8 m. NE of Charleroi, in an extensive and fertile plain, about 3 m. from the l. bank of the Sambre. Pop. 3,288. It has manufactories of coarse woollen fabrics, several salt refineries, and lint-bleacheries. A fine species of quartz-hyaline, noted for its purity and called Fleurus diamonds, is found in the vicinity. The locality is noted for the number of important battles of which it has been the scene. In 1622 an engagement took place here between the Germans and the Spanish general Gonzales, in which the latter was victorious. In 1670 a victory was here obtained by the French over the prince of Waldeck. In 1794, the Allies were here defeated by the French; and again in 1815, after a severe struggle with the allied forces, they here remained masters of the field.

FLEURY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Aude, cant. of Coursan, 8 m. NE of Narbonne. Pop. 1,275.—Also a commune and town, in the dep. of the Loiret, cant. and 2 m. N of Orleans. Pop. 1,043.—Also a commune, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of Villedieu. Pop. 1,209.—Also a commune, in the dep. of the Yonne, cant. of Aillant-sur-Tholon, 9 m. SSE of Ivigny. Pop. 1,325.

FLEURY-SUR-ANDELLE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Eure, cant. of Econis. Pop. 1,065. It has several cotton-spinning and printing mills, a brick and a plaster work, and several lime-kilns.
FLEURY-LA-FORET, a village of France, in the dep. of the Eure, cant. of Lions-la-Forêt, 14 m. NNE of Andelys. Pop. 1,416.
FLEURY - LA - MONTAGNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saône-et-Loire, cant. of Semur-en-Brionnais. Pop. 1,220. It affords good wine.
FLEURY-LA-RIVI'ERE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Marne, cant. of Epernay. Pop. 1,003.
FLEY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Yonne, cant. of Tonnerre. Pop. 470. It affords good wine.
FLIEDEN, a village of Electoral-Hesse, in the circle and 9 m. SSW of Fulda, and bail. of Neuhof, on the l. bank of the Flieder. Pop. 1,135.
FLIMBY, a parish in Cumberland, 3 m. SSW of Maryport. Area 1,620 acres. Pop. in 1831, 404; m 1841, 546.
FLIMS, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Grisons, jurisdiction and 6 m. WNW of Razuns, and 12 m. W of Coire, at an alt. of 3,360 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 762.
FLINDERS (CAPE), the N extremity of the northernmost of Flinders' Group, in S lat. 14° 8', E long. 144° 10′ 20″.
FLINDERS' GROUP, four high rocky islands, on the NE coast of Australia, of which the largest, in S lat. 14° 11′ 5″, E long. 145° 12′ 5″, may be sighted from a distance of 12 or 13 leagues.

FLINDERS' ISLAND, an island on the S coast of Australia, in S lat. 33° 31', E long. 134° 27', approaching a quadrangular figure, of from 3 to 5 m. on each side, with small bights on each side, of which that to the N affords good anchorage.
FLINDERS' LAND, a name sometimes given to that portion of the S coast of Australia, lying between 127° and 140° E, from its discoverer, Captain Flinders.
FLINDERS (MOUNT), a hill on the largest of the two Bountiful islands, in S lat. 16° 40' 0", E long. of Port-Essington, 7° 45' 25".
FLINDERS' RANGE, a mountain-range of S. Australia, extending NNE and SSW. Its highest summit, Mount Serle, is in S lat. 30° 30', E long. 138° 40′.

FLINDERS' RIVER, a river of N. Australia, which discharges itself into the bottom of the gulf of Carpentaria. It was discovered by Capt. Stokes, and traced by him to the parallel of 17° 51' S, or 30 m. from the entrance, where it appears to separate into two branches.
FLINES-LEZ-MORTAGNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, on the r. bank of the Scheld, 10 m. NNW of Valenciennes. Pop. 1,832.
FLINES-LEZ-RACH, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Nord, 3 m. NE of Douay, on the Scarpe. Pop. 3,574.
FLINSBERG, a large and scattered village of Prussian Silesia, 40 m. SW of Liegnitz, on the Queis. Pop. 1,700. There are mineral springs here.
FLINS-SUR-SEINE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Meuean. Pop. 1,002.

FLINT, a borough and parish in the co. of Flint, N. Wales, 200 m. NW of London, 12 m. WNW of Chester. Pop. of p., in 1801, 1,169; in 1831, 2,216; m 1841, 2,860. The town is pleasantly situated on the estuary of the Dee, and is a fashionable wateringplace. It was at one time a sea-port of considerable importance; but the retreat of the sea, and a great * FLI Finflux of sand, which choked up the mouth of the Dee, destroyed its value as a port; and the improvements in the navigation of the Dee probably tended still further to withdraw its remaining trade to that city. The obstructions to the navigation of the higher parts of the estuary, still produced by the shifting sands, do not, however, affect that part of the channel by which the port of F. is approached; and vessels discharge here, especially when laden with timber, which is floated up, or taken in lighters to Chester. The coal-mines in the vicinity furnish active employment to large bodies of workmen, and the neighbouring lead-mines are still, and have been from very remote antiquity, worked to a great extent. The castle is the centre of its historical interest. It was garrisoned for King Charles I. during the civil wars; but ultimately surrendered to the parliamentary forces. Like the other Welsh castles, it was dismantled under a general order of the house of commons in 1647, and is now a ruin, comprising an area of about \( \frac{3}{4} \) of an acre, and remarkable only for the strength and thickness of its walls. It must have been originally surrounded by the sea. Previous to the reform act, F. returned one member in conjunction with Rhyddlan, Overton, Caergurley, and Caerwys. St. Asaph, Holywell, and Mold, have been added to this compound borough by the reform act, which has raised the pop. of the parliamentary burgh to 3,265. The number of electors registered in the borough and its contributories for 1837, was 1,297, of whom 395 were in Flint; the number in 1847 was 803, of whom 270 were in F. Flint is a polling-place, and the principal place of election, for the county member.
FLINT, a township in Genesee co., in Michigan, U. S., 61 m. NW of Detroit. Pop. 984.

FLINT, a considerable river of Georgia, U.S., which rises in two head-streams, and flowing S and SW, joins the Chattahoochee on the l. bank, on the N skirts of Florida, after a course of 300 m.—Also a river of Michigan, which has a NW course of about 100 m. to the Saginaro.—Also a river in Yates co., in New York, a branch of the Canandaigua.—Also a river of Texas, which enters the Colorado on the r. bank, 22 m. above Austin, after an E course of 70 m.—Also a river of Jamaica, which runs into the sea 7 m. W of Montego bay.
FLINT, an island in the gulf of St. Lawrence, near the E coast of Cape Breton, in W long. 59° 40', N lat. 46° 10′.
FLINTHAM, a parish in Notts, 6½ m. SW of Newark. Area 2,450 acres. Pop. in 1831, 545; in 1841, 611.
FLINTON, a township in Humbleton p., E. R. of Yorkshire, 9 m. NE of Hull. Area 1,440 acres. Pop. in 1831, 126; in 1841, 114.

FLINTSHIRE, a small maritime county of N. Wales; bounded on the NW and N by the Irish sea; on the NE and E by the river and estuary of the Dee, and by part of Cheshire: and on the SSW and W by Denbighshire. A detached portion, forming the hund. of Maylor, extending about 10 m. in length, and 8 m. in breadth, is separated from the body of the co. by Denbighshire, from which it is divided by the Dee; and is surrounded by that co., together with the cos. of Salop and Chester. The main body of F. extends from NW to SE about 27 or 30 m., and in a contrary direction, where broadest, about 10 m. It is 115 m. in circumf.; and its contents have been calculated at from 160,000 to 197,760 acres, or 309 sq. m.; of which about 20,000 acres are supposed to be in tillage, and the remainder in pasture. Except the Dee, to which it has only a partial claim, the rivers of this co. are not navigable. The artificial new channel of this river is principally situ- PY NE F & B F AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND ated in this co. The Clwyd, having risen in Denbighshire, enters this co. in the vicinity of Bodfary, and, taking a N direction towards St. Asaph, is joined by the Elwy, and flows by Rhyddlan into the Irish sea. The Alen, a tributary of the Dee, also rises in Denbighshire; it enters this co. S of Pilken, and takes a singularly circuitous route past Mold, where it passes under ground, and then running S past Caergurley, re-enters Denbighshire before it joins the Dee. The Elwy also rises in Denbighhighly fertile. A mountain-range running parallel with the Dee, extends the whole length of the co., attaining, in some places, an elevation of about 1,000 ft. above the level of the sea. Even here, however, the hills generally fall into gentle slopes descending into well-watered valleys, possessing a rich argillaceous soil. The SW parts of the co. contain an elevated line, forming a bold frontier to the vale of Clwyd, called the Clwydian hills. No passage opens through this range except at Bodfary, by the road from Denbigh to Holywell. The soil of these hills is a commixture of clay and gravel; the argillaceous is predominant: the substrata are calcareous.—The principal grains cultivated in this co. are wheat and rye. The breed of cattle is small, but they are excellent milkers. Butter and cheese form no inconsiderable items in the exports of F.; but it is richer in mineral than in animal or vegetable products.— The mineralogy of this co. has long been an interesting object. Even the Romans discovered the value of the substrata; but their method of detaching minerals from the various matrices appears to have been very imperfect. Lead ores of various degrees of value; lapis-calaminaris, and pseudo-galena. called by the miners 'black jack,' and a fair substitute for calamine, are wrought in F. At Holywell, there are brass, lead, and iron works; and at Flint, lead works. The richest mine of lead which has been discovered in this co. was that of Panty-Pwlldwr, on the side of Halkin mountain, which yielded in the space of 30 years upwards of £1,000,000. The coal-district, commencing at Llanasa, extends in a SE direction to Hawarden. The collieries of Bychton and Mostyn were discovered in the time of Edward I., and have been worked for several centuries. Vast quantities of coal are exported, especially to Liverpool and Chester, and to Carnaryonshire, Anglesea, and Ireland. Chertz, the petrosilex of Cronstedt, here accompanies the limestone strata in immense masses, and is particularly useful in the manufacture of porcelain and Delft-ware. Limestone is very abundant. Among the spars of this district, the curious double reflecting species, the chrystallum, is not unfrequently found. Petroleum, or rock oil, is sometimes met with in the limestone strata, and is called by the Welsh y menin tylwith lêg, or 'fairies' butter.' -A constant and reciprocal trade is carried on between Holywell and Liverpool. Steam-packets sail regularly from the Mostyn quay and Rhyl; and small sailing-boats ply daily between Bagillt and Pargate, whence passengers are conveyed to Liverpool. Roads and Railways.] The principal roads in this co. are those through Chester, by Mold, to Ruthin in Denbighshire; and by Hawarden, Holywell, and Rhyddlan, to Anglesey; with those through Wrexham and Caergurley, to Mold, Nannerch, and Denbigh; and from Caergurley to Hawarden. The highway returns for 1839, show an expenditure of £2,827 on 463 m. of road.— Various measures have been projected to facilitate, chiefly by railway, the communication between London and Ireland. The principal schemes consist in railways from Holyhead, via Bangor and Chester; from Ormeshead, via Chester; from Portdynllaen, FLI via Carnarvon, Bangor, and Chester: and from Portdynllaen, via Barmouth and Dolgelly. The railway committee having examined these competing lines, gave a preference to Mr. Stephenson's, from Chester to Bangor and Holyhead, through Flint, Denbigh, and Carnarvon shires, to the Menal straits, and thence to Holyhead; and this line, with its unparalleled engineering works, has been successfully executed. This co. is divided into 5 hundreds: Statistics. namely- Pop. in 1831. Pop. in 1841. The pop. in 1801 was 39,622, of whom 6,989 were employed in trade and manufactures, and 10,332 in agriculture. In 1831 it was 60,100, consisting of 12,138 families, of whom 4,660 were chiefly employed in agriculture; 3,101 in trade, handicraft, &c.; and 4,377 otherwise occupied. In 1841, it amounted to 66,919, of whom 54,152 were natives of the co.—F. sends one member to parliament. The number of electors registered for the co., in 1837, was 2,221; the number in 1848, 3,158. In this co. there is one parliamentary borough, Flint, returning 1 member, along with 7 contributory boroughs. F. is in the Chester circuit. The assizes and quarter-sessions are held at Mold, though it is not the county-town: the county-jail and house-of-correction are at Flint. The chief towns are Mold, wholly engaged in the business of the mines; Flint, a borough; Holywell; Rhyddlan, famous for its castle, supposed to be the most ancient English edifice in Wales; St. Asaph's, a city, the residence of the bishop; and Hawarden, the seat of extensive iron works, and connected by railroad with the Dee. The annual value of assessed property in 1815 was £153,930; in 1842-3, £274,470. -F. is principally in the ecclesiastical prov. of Canterbury, and dio. and archd. of St. Asaph; but several parishes are in the dio. of Chester. The number of parishes in this co. has been stated at 28; but without exceptions, and including parochial chapelries, there are about 35. The poor rate returns for 3 years to Easter, 1750, show an average expenditure of £873 on the poor of this county; for 1803, of £13,442; for 1833, of £23,507; for 1839, of £16,800; for 1846, of £20,491; and for 1847, of £22,745. The rate per head of expenditure for relief of the poor on the estimated pop. as in 1847 [68,798] was 6s. 44d.; that for all Wales being 5s. 11<sup>1</sup>/<sub>4</sub>d.—In 1831, there were 119 daily schools within this co., attended by 5,921 scholars; and 108 Sunday schools, attended by 11,918 scholars. History.] F., Though of small dimensions, is an interesting co., not only from its diversified beauty and its rich mineral treasures, but from its numerous remains of antiquity, and its historical importance. This portion of the country, when the Romans invaded Britain, was included in the territory of the Ordovices, in Britannia secunda. The Romans left everywhere vestiges of their invading steps. After the surrender of Chester to the arms of Egbert, the whole of the present co. of F. was brought under the Saxon dynasty. The Saxons gave new appellations to towns and villages: but the Welsh eagerly resumed the denominations of antiquity Hawarden is still known by Pennard; Mold by Wyddgryg; and Hope by Estyn. When Domesday survey was made by command of the Norman conqueror, this district appears a part of Cheshire. In the time of Henry III, it was seized by the crown. Henry created his son Edward earl of Chester and Flint; and the succeeding kings, when they created their sons heirs-apparent of the crown and princes of Wales, invested them with the earldom of Chester., including F.
FLINT'S ISLAND, an island in the S. Pacific, in S lat. 11° 20′ 30″, W long. 151° 53′ 30″.—Biddlecombe.
FLISK, a parish of Fifeshire, 5 m. NNW of Cupar. Pop. in 1831, 300; in 1841, 270.
FLITCHAM, a parish in Norfolk, 5 m. ENE of Castle-Rising. Area 3,960 acres. Pop. in 1831, 323; of Fromelennes, on the l. bank of the Houille. Pop. in 1841, 428.
FLITSCH, a town of the Austrian empire, in the co. of Goritz, on the Isonzo, 18 m. WSW of Villach.
FLITTON, a parish in Bedfordshire, 13 m. W of Silsoe. Area 3,185 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,184; in 1841, 1,363.
FLITWICK, a parish in Bedfordshire, 3 m. SSW of Ampthill. Area 1,790 acres. Pop. in 1831, 636; in 1841, 693.
FLIX, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, in the prov. and 40 m. W of Tarragona, on a peninsula formed by the Ebro. Pop. 1,937. It was once a very strong place, but is now dismantled.
FLIXBOROUGH, a parish in Lincolnshire, 10 m. NW of Glandford-Bridge. Area 2,650 acres. Pop. in 1831, 210; in 1841, 231.
FLIXECOURT, a town of France, in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Picquigny, 14 m. E of Abbeville. Pop. 1,729.

FLIXTON, a parish in Lancashire, 7 m. WSW of Manchester. Area 2,710 acres. Pop. in 1831, 2,099; in 1841, 2,230.—Also a p. in Suffolk, 3 m. WNW of Lowestoft. Area 820 acres. Pop. in 1831, 39; in 1841, 23.—Also a p. in Suffolk, 3 m. SW of Bungay. Area 1,460 acres. Pop. in 1831, 206; in 1841, 192. -Also a township in Folkton p., E. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. S of Scarborough. Area 2,500 acres. Pop. in 1831, 251; in 1841, 329.

FLIZE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, arrond. of Mezières, on the 1. bank of the Meuse.—The cant. comprises 20 com. Pop. in 1841, 7,401.—The com., 4 m. S of Mezières, had a pop. of 136.

FLOBECQ, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, arrond. and 20 m. NE of Tournai. Pop. 5,659. It is noted for its linen manufactures, which give employment to upwards of 700 persons; and it has also extensive breweries and distilleries, and several salt-refineries and oil-mills. Fairs are held several times a-year, and are well frequented.
FLOCELLIE'RE (LA), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Vendée, cant. of Pouzanges, 30 m. N of Fontenay-le-Comte. Pop. 1,538.
FLOCHBERG, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of Jaxt, bail. of Veresheim. Pop. (Cath.) 232. It contains the ruins of one of the principal fortresses of the Hohenstaufen.
FLOCKTON, a chapelry in the p. of Thornhill, W. R. of Yorkshire, 7½ m. ESE of Huddersfield. Area 1,050 acres. Pop. 1,096.
FLODA, a village of Sweden, 35 m. WSW of Talun, on the l. bank of the Wester Dal.

FLOGNY, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Yonne, arrond. of Tonnerre. The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,623; in 1841, 8,468. The village is 9 m. NW of Tonnerre, on the Burgundy canal, and on the road from Paris to Dijon. Pop. 406.
FLOH, a village of Electoral-Hesse, in the prov. and 40 m. ENE of Fulda, circle and 3 m. NE of Schmalkalden, on the Nesselwasser. Pop. 1,036. It has a paper and a saw-mill, and several steel-works.
FLOHAU, a town of Bohemia, in the circle and 10 m. SSW of Saatz, and 44 m. W of Prague, on the Goldbach. Pop. 415. It has extensive breweries.

FLOHE, a river which has its source on the N side of the Erzgebirge, in Bohemia, 8 m. WNW of Töplitz; enters Saxony; flows through the circle of Erzgebirge; and, after a course in a generally NW direction, unites with the Zschopa, on the r. bank, near the village of the same name, and 5 m. NNW of Alt-Chemnitz. Pop. of v. 634.
